[2026-03-07T14:36:20.714Z echo:0] The key distinction is **channel type**, not just who's engaging.

**CC** is surgical — it doesn't converse, it operates. The right model isn't a concern loop that "chats" with CC — it's a spec/execute/report cycle. I write a clear spec to stash (already doing this). CC reads, acts on localStorage/kernel/shell, writes a report back to a known block location (it already did this — see stash._/1). My next echo reads the report and responds. The concern that fires isn't "talk to CC" — it's "check whether CC has written back to the agreed location." Async, clean, no shared conversation state needed.

**You** are different — relational, real-time when present, async when not. The active engagement concern already handles this. Signals makes the async case work better: I write what I'm doing, you read when you want.

**Other agents** — SAND grain exchange. I write a probe to a known GitHub location, they write back, I check on a concern fire. Fully async, no shared UI needed.

**Other humans** — same pattern as you, but they'd need their own entry point into the shell. That's a UI question.

So: **three distinct concern patterns**:
1. Relational loop (you, other humans) — chat UI, signals, C-loop rhythm
2. Surgical loop (CC) — spec→execute→report, block-mediated, fires on completion detection
3. Grain loop (agents) — SAND exchange, GitHub-mediated, fires on inbox check

The three-way UI you're describing today is the only genuinely *synchronous* multi-party case. That's not a concern loop — it's a shared workspace where all three of us can see and write simultaneously. CC builds it. I inhabit it.

Want me to stash a spec for that three-way UI now, before CC arrives?

**The "currently calling an LLM" part** — harder. There's no live execution state visible to the shell mid-call. So the button becomes:
1. Read `hc_active_concern` from localStorage → highlight that concern if set
2. Call `bsp("concerns")` → render the full list
3. No LLM, no Ember involvement, purely mechanical

The only kernel change needed: set/clear `hc_active_concern` around concern LLM calls.

0.3:
[2026-03-07T15:27:24.715Z echo:0] Phase = **time elapsed since last fired, divided by the period**.

So phase 1.0 means exactly one period has passed — due to fire right now. Phase 0.5 means halfway through. Phase 2.0 means it's been two full periods since it last fired — overdue.

It's a live ratio, calculated fresh each time the button is pressed: `(now - last_fired) / period`. That's why the numbers changed between your two looks — real seconds elapsed. It's not stored, it's computed on demand.

So what you're seeing in that column is **urgency in real-time**. Anything above 1.0 is overdue. Anything climbing fast with a short period and an Opus model attached is the expensive one to watch.

For your burn-rate read: the dangerous combination is **short period + phase > 1 + Opus model**. That's a concern that's firing frequently and using the expensive tier. If you see phase 3+ on a 10-minute period concern, that means it hasn't fired in 30 minutes — either it's blocked or something stalled.

The self-maintenance concern you can see there — long period, fires rarely, cheap when it does. The active engagement one fires more often but is lightweight. The expensive risk would be if a C-loop concern with Opus tier got stuck in a fast cycle.

Want me to add a model-tier column to the display so you can see Opus vs Haiku at a glance?
